If the system needs trusted knowledge or policy information beyond what the LLM was trained on, then I use a RAG-
based system, because retrieval from approved documents is more reliable, auditable and controllable than relying 
on the LLM memory alone, using vector retrieval, BM25 retrieval or Hybrid RAG to ensure grounded and explainable 
answers. 
If exact wording or precise terminology matters, then I include BM25 or keyword retrieval, because semantic 
retrieval alone may miss exact phrases, policy terms or identifiers, using Azure AI Search, OpenSearch or 
Elasticsearch to ensure precision and trustworthiness. 
If knowledge exists in multiple retrieval forms such as semantic similarity, keyword matching and structured 
relationships, then I use Hybrid RAG, because combining retrieval strategies improves

ss. 
If knowledge exists in multiple retrieval forms such as semantic similarity, keyword matching and structured 
relationships, then I use Hybrid RAG, because combining retrieval strategies improves recall, ranking and retrieval 
robustness, using vector DBs together with BM25 indexes to ensure better evidence coverage. 
If relationships between entities, routes, locations or dependencies are important, then I use Graph RAG, because 
graph reasoning naturally models connected systems such as transport networks, healthcare pathways or geospatial 
relationships, using Neo4j, Cosmos DB Gremlin or Neptune to ensure intelligent traversal and optimisation. 
If the system needs access to APIs, tools or operational systems, then I use Agentic RAG, because the agent must 
coordinate retrieval, reasoning and tool invocation rather than only generate text, using LangGraph, LangChain or 
tool routers to ensure orchestrated workflow execution. 
If standardised access to external tools and APIs is important, then I consider MCP-based orchestration, because 
MCP helps unify communication between tools and agents using standard interfaces to ensure cleaner integration 
and interoperability.

If user preferences, historical behaviour or workflow context matter, then I use Contextual RAG, because the system 
must remember and reuse user-related information during the interaction, using workflow state DBs, Redis caches 
and contextual retrieval to ensure personalised and state-aware responses.

If there are sensitive, restricted or regulated operations such as payments, appointments, bookings or healthcare 
records, then I keep those processes deterministic and backend-controlled, because operational truth, privacy and 
compliance must remain outside LLM reasoning, using secure APIs, backend workflows and databases to ensure 
governance and safety. 
If the workflow involves multiple steps such as search, refinement, comparison, confirmation, booking, payment and 
notification, then the system naturally becomes stateful and orchestration-driven, because each step depends on 
previous workflow state and user decisions, using LangGraph orchestration and workflow state DBs to ensure 
controlled progression. 
If workflow state exists such as booking state, appointment state, trip state, audit state or confirmation state, then 
it must remain externalised outside pods and LLM memory, because pods may restart or autoscale and LLM memory 
is not reliable persistent storage,

If a component mainly processes APIs, retrieval or ranking requests, then I keep it mostly stateless, because stateless 
services scale horizontally much more easily, using Kubernetes HPA or ECS autoscaling to ensure efficient scalability. 
If the architecture contains both stateful and stateless components, then I explicitly separate them early in the 
design, because scaling, persistence and failure handling behave very differently for each type, using external 
databases for stateful data and autoscaled replicas for stateless services to ensure resilience and operational 
simplicity. 
If operations can execute independently or concurrently, then I use asynchronous orchestration and parallel tool 
execution, because waiting synchronously for every operation increases latency and reduces scalability, using Python 
async/await, queues and worker pods to ensure efficient concurrency.

request path, then I use asynchronous queues and workers, because background workloads should be decoupled 
from interactive workflows, using Azure Service Bus, Kafka, Event Hubs or SQS to ensure smoother traffic handling. 
If the platform becomes highly event-driven with continuous updates and telemetry streams, then queues, event 
buses and streaming systems become core architectural components, because synchronous blocking workflows 
cannot efficiently manage high-frequency operational events, using Kafka, Event Hubs, Kinesis or Service Bus to 
ensure scalable event processing.

If queueing becomes necessary, then I identify which tasks require immediate response and which tasks can be 
processed asynchronously, because not every operation needs synchronous execution, using queue workers and 
scaling rules based on queue depth and latency targets to ensure efficient workload distribution. 
If operations are operationally sensitive such as booking, payment, dispatch or healthcare scheduling, then I isolate 
those services and keep them deterministic, because transactional workflows require stronger guarantees than 
conversational workflows, using dedicated backend services and controlled state transitions to ensure reliability.

worker pods, because independent workers process streaming events more efficiently than synchronous APIs, using 
Kafka consumers or EventHub consumers to ensure scalable stream processing. 
If the platform combines AI reasoning, graph traversal, retrieval, telemetry and asynchronous workflows, then 
distributed tracing and structured observability become mandatory, because failures may occur across many 
interacting components, using structlog, correlation IDs, traces and central logging platforms to ensure traceability. 
If updates, notifications or retries should not block APIs, then I process them asynchronously, because API

If the system introduces self-hosted LLMs, rerankers, computer vision or heavy ML inference, then GPU

If runtime behaviour and operational load are important, then I carefully distinguish what can run asynchronously

If there are operational updates or real-time state changes such as GPS telemetry, rail delays or ride dispatch updates, 
then I design the platform as event-driven, because continuous polling and synchronous coordination become

If graph optimisation or network traversal becomes central to the business logic, then graph databases become more

If reliability and debugging are important in distributed systems, then I use structured logging, exception handling 
and central observability, because distributed failures are difficult to trace without consistent telemetry, using 
try/except handling, structlog, StreamHandler, OpenTelemetry and central logging platforms to ensure 
debuggability. 
If deployment reproducibility and operational consistency are important, then I externalise runtime definitions into 
manifests and infrastructure-as-code, because environments must remain reproducible across development, staging 
and production, using Kubernetes YAML, Terraform, Bicep or CloudFormation to ensure portability and repeatability. 
If scalability, resilience and operational isolation become important, then I gradually evolve from modular monolith 
toward selected microservices, because some workloads eventually require independent scaling and ownership 
boundaries, using service separation and orchestration platforms to ensure maintainability and enterprise scalability.

Security and privacy: 
Add these rules to the same rule-based narrative: 
If users, staff or services have different permissions, then I use RBAC, because not every user or component should 
access the same data or actions, using Entra ID/IAM roles, service accounts and least-privilege policies to ensure 
controlled access. 


[Page 4]
If the system handles sensitive personal, health, payment or location data, then I apply data minimisation, because 
the platform should only collect and store what is necessary, using scoped schemas, retention rules and access 
policies to reduce privacy risk. 
If identity matters, then I use strong authentication and authorisation, because appointment booking, payment, ride 
assignment or ticketing must be tied to the correct user, using OAuth2/OIDC, Entra ID, Cognito, NHS identity 
integration or verified mobile identity. 
If service-to-service communication is required, then I use managed identities or workload identities, because 
services should not rely on hardcoded credentials, using Azure Managed Identity, IAM roles, Key Va

-to-service communication is required, then I use managed identities or workload identities, because 
services should not rely on hardcoded credentials, using Azure Managed Identity, IAM roles, Key Vault or Secrets 
Manager to ensure secure access. 
If secrets, API keys or payment credentials are needed, then I never store them in code, because leaked credentials 
can compromise the whole platform, using Key Vault, Secrets Manager, environment injection and secret rotation. 
If payment, booking, appointment or ticketing requests may be retried, then I use idempotency keys, because retries 
must not create duplicate payments, bookings, appointments or tickets, using unique request IDs stored in the 
transaction database and passed to provider APIs. 
If a user must approve an action, then I use confirmation states, because the LLM should not autonomously execute 
sensitive operations, using backend state machines such as awaiting_confirmation, confirmed, cancelled, paid or 
booked. 
If the system processes regulated or high-risk operations, then I keep those operations deterministic, because 
auditability and safety are more important than free-form AI behaviour, using backend rules,

he system processes regulated or high-risk operations, then I keep those operations deterministic, because 
auditability and safety are more important than free-form AI behaviour, using backend rules, validated APIs and 
explicit state transitions. 
If the LLM suggests an action, then I validate it before execution, because AI output may be incomplete or unsafe, 
using schemas, policy checks, business rules, source-of-truth validation and human confirmation. 
If personal data appears in prompts or logs, then I redact or minimise it, because logs and prompts can become 
privacy risks, using PII filtering, structured logging policies and safe prompt construction. 
If auditability is required, then I log decisions, tool calls, confirmations and state changes, because regulated systems 
need traceability, using audit tables, correlation IDs, workflow IDs and immutable log storage. 
If the system uses external APIs, then I apply timeout, retry and circuit-breaker policies, because failures should not 
expose sensitive state or cause duplicate actions, using controlled API adapters and monitored fallback paths.

If different services run in the same cluster, then I isolate them logically, because one compromised or overloaded

If databases contain sensitive data, then I encrypt data at rest and in transit, because storage and network traffic

If data has different sensitivity levels, then I separate storage and access paths, because public guidance, user state, 
payment records and audit logs should not share the same access model, using separate databases, 
containers/buckets and access policies.

Additional Recommended Rules 
Trust Boundaries & Source-of-Truth Governance 
If multiple systems provide overlapping information, then I explicitly define the source of truth for each domain, 
because conflicting operational data creates inconsistency and unsafe behaviour, using bounded ownership such as 
scheduling systems for appointments, rail APIs for delays and payment systems for transaction state. 
If AI-generated recommendations affect operational workflows, then I validate them against live operational 
systems before execution, because retrieval or reasoning may become stale or incomplete, using validation services 
and operational API verification layers. 
If operational data may become stale, delayed or partially unavailable, then I include freshness validation and 
confidence indicators, because users must know

d operational API verification layers. 
If operational data may become stale, delayed or partially unavailable, then I include freshness validation and 
confidence indicators, because users must know whether information is live, delayed or estimated, using timestamps, 
TTL policies, freshness scores and degraded-mode messaging. 
 
Graceful Degradation & Failure Modes 
If external dependencies fail or become slow, then the platform should degrade gracefully rather than fully fail, 
because distributed systems must continue operating under partial outages, using cached guidance, fallback 
workflows, retry queues and degraded operational modes. 
If live APIs become unavailable, then I provide fallback guidance and partial functionality where safe, because 
operational continuity is better than complete service failure, using cached policies, delayed retries and user-visible 
degraded-state messaging. 


[Page 6]
If event streams become delayed or inconsistent, then I apply reconciliation workflows, because distributed systems 
may experience eventual consistency and out-of-order updates, using reconciliation workers, replay logic and state-
correction jobs.

Human Override & Governance 
If operational recommendations may impact safety, compliance or public operations, then humans or operational 
systems remain the final authority, because AI assistance must not override regulated operational governance, using 
approval checkpoints, operator override workflows and backend validation layers. 
If AI confidence is low or evidence is weak, then the system should escalate, defer or request clarification, because 
low-confidence automation increases operational risk, using confidence thresholds, fallback workflows and human-
review escalation. 
If recommendations affect fairness, accessibility or public service availability, then I apply policy-aware ranking rules, 
because optimisation alone may unintentionally disadvantage users or regions, using governance constraints and 
explainable ranking policies.

workflows logically or p

Multi-Tenancy & Isolation 
If the platform serves multiple organisations, providers or operational regions, then I isolate tenant data and 
workflows logically or physically, because tenant crossover creates security and governance risks, using tenant-
aware schemas, namespaces, RBAC and isolated storage boundaries. 
If environments differ across development, staging and production, then I isolate configuration and secrets per 
environment, because cross-environment leakage can create operational and security failures, using environment-
scoped IAM, vaults and deployment pipelines. 
 
Data Governance & Lifecycle 
If embeddings, indexes or prompts evolve over time, then I version AI artefacts explicitly, because retrieval behaviour 
and AI responses must remain reproducible and auditable, using embedding-version metadata, prompt registries 
and index versioning. 
If models, prompts or ranking strategies change in production, then I evaluate them before rollout, because AI 
regressions may silently degrade system quality, using offline evaluation datasets, shadow testing and staged 
deployments.

deployments. 
If operational telemetry grows continuously, then I separate hot operational data from long-term analytics storage, 
because real-time systems and historical analytics have different latency and retention requirements, using 
hot/warm/cold architecture. 
 
SLO/SLA & Runtime Governance 
If the platform supports real-time operational workflows, then I define SLOs and latency budgets early, because 
architecture decisions depend on acceptable response times and failure thresholds, using API latency targets, queue 
lag targets and uptime objectives. 
If some workloads are more critical than others, then I prioritise queues and runtime resources, because 
operationally critical workflows must survive traffic spikes, using priority queues, QoS rules and workload isolation. 
If runtime cost may grow rapidly with scale, then I continuously monitor AI and infrastructure consumption, because 
uncontrolled inference and orchestration costs can become operationally unsustainable, using token monitoring, 
autoscaling policies and budget alerts.

Security & Compliance Extensions 
If the system operates across countries or regulated regions, then I enforce data residency and regional governance 
boundaries, because legal compliance may restrict where data is processed or stored, using regional deployments 
and geo-scoped storage. 
If APIs are exposed to third parties or partners, then I apply scoped API contracts and rate governance, because 
external integrations increase operational and abuse risk, using API gateways, quotas, signed tokens and usage 
policies. 
If AI prompts may contain operational instructions or sensitive context, then I protect against prompt injection and 
tool misuse, because external content may manipulate orchestration behaviour, using tool whitelisting, prompt 
isolation and retrieval sanitisation. 
If the platform uses tool-calling agents, then I restrict tool permissions per workflow context, because not every 
workflow should access every operational capability, using scoped tool registrie

n.

workflow should access every operational capability, using scoped tool registries and policy-aware orchestration. 
 
Operational AI Governance 
If retrieval evidence is weak, conflicting or incomplete, then the system should communicate uncertainty explicitly, 
because users should distinguish between verified operational truth and probabilistic AI reasoning, using evidence 
scoring and confidence-aware responses. 
If the AI system affects operational decisions, then I continuously evaluate hallucination, grounding and ranking 
quality, because production AI quality drifts over time, using RAGAS, DeepEval, human review datasets and 
operational feedback loops. 
If enterprise adoption grows, then I evolve from experimentation toward governed AI lifecycle management, 
because production AI requires operational discipline, using CI/CD, evaluation gates, model registries and LLMOps 
governance.

Additional Rules for Graph RAG Construction and Integration 
If the business problem depends on relationships, routes, dependencies, hierarchies, causal chains, or network 
traversal, then I introduce a graph layer, because vector retrieval alone cannot reliably represent how entities are 
connected, using Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, Cosmos DB Gremlin, TigerGraph, or a property-graph layer. 
If the graph is being introduced, then I first define the graph purpose, because not every system needs the same 
graph, using domain questions such as route optimisation, root-cause analysis, nearby alternatives, referral 
pathways, dependency tracing, or component impact analysis.

Simple rule summary 
If it is transactional → relational DB. 
 
If it is flexible workflow/user state → NoSQL/document DB. 
 
If it is semantic knowledge → vector DB. 
 
If it is exact keyword/policy retrieval → BM25 index. 
 
If it is connected entities/routes/networks → graph DB. 
 
If it is fast temporary state → Redis/cache. 
 
If it is a document/file/archive → Blob/S3. 
 
If it is async work → queue. 
 
If it is continuous events → event stream. 
 
If it is compliance evidence → audit log store. 
 
If it is monitoring data → metrics/time-s
